text: A gel point port is inserted through a 3.5 cm umbilical cutaneous incision, and a 2 mm mini lap needle scopic grasper is placed in the right lower quadrant. The surgeon performs primary vascular control with complete mesocolic excision and central vascular ligation along the right border of the superior mesenteric vein. An avascular surgical plane, known as Toldt's line, is exposed to reveal the duodenum.

The intraoperative view shows several enlarged lymph nodes in the right colic mesentery near the central vascular trunk. The surgeon performs lymph node dissection along the superior mesenteric artery and vein.

The surgeon ligates and transects the iliocolic vein at its root, where it drains into the superior mesenteric vein. The iliocolic artery is also transected at its root and dissected upwards towards the middle colic vessels.

The surgeon exposes an avascular surgical plane to reveal the head of the pancreas and duodenum. Following lymph node dissection around the middle colic vessels, the right branch of the middle colic artery is identified, ligated, and transected at its root.

The surgeon incises the peritoneum in the retrocecal recess and mobilizes it laterally. The greater omentum of the transverse colon is transected, and the attachment at the hepatic flexure is detached.

The final intraoperative view shows the surgical field after complete mesocolic excision and central vascular ligation for right-sided colon cancer, with clear exposure of the anatomical structures and absence of visible bleeding.
A 2mm incision is made in the right lower quadrant to facilitate drainage.
